title: A Decisional 55-Year-Old Jehovah's Witness with an Acute Upper-GI Bleed and Hemoglobin 6.5 Declines Blood Transfusion on Religious Grounds: Honoring the Informed Refusal of a Competent Adult, Clarifying Exactly Which Products/Techniques He Accepts, Maximizing Bloodless (Patient-Blood-Management) Alternatives, Avoiding Coercion or Covert Transfusion, and the Different Rule for a Minor Child
subtitle: A COMPETENT (decisional) ADULT has the legal and ethical right to REFUSE any treatment — INCLUDING a potentially life-saving BLOOD TRANSFUSION — on religious or any other grounds, even when refusal may result in death; AUTONOMY and INFORMED CONSENT/REFUSAL govern, and respecting this refusal is required (forcing or covertly administering blood on a competent refusing adult is battery and a profound ethical/legal violation). JEHOVAH'S WITNESSES: based on religious belief, they decline transfusion of WHOLE BLOOD and the FOUR PRIMARY COMPONENTS — RED CELLS, WHITE CELLS, PLATELETS, and PLASMA. However, decisions about minor BLOOD FRACTIONS (e.g., albumin, clotting factors, immunoglobulins, cryoprecipitate-derived fractions) and about AUTOLOGOUS techniques (intraoperative CELL SALVAGE, acute normovolemic hemodilution, dialysis/cardiopulmonary bypass circuits) are matters of INDIVIDUAL CONSCIENCE and VARY from person to person — so the nurse/team must ASK THE INDIVIDUAL PATIENT specifically what they WILL and WILL NOT accept rather than assuming; many patients carry a signed ADVANCE DIRECTIVE / 'no-blood' card documenting their precise wishes (which also guides care if they later lose consciousness). NURSING/ETHICAL APPROACH: (1) ASSESS DECISION-MAKING CAPACITY and ensure the refusal is INFORMED — the patient understands the diagnosis, the recommended transfusion and its benefits, the RISKS of refusing (including severe anemia, organ injury, and DEATH), and the alternatives; capacity is presumed in adults and is NOT negated by making a choice others consider unwise. (2) CLARIFY + DOCUMENT exactly which products/fractions/techniques the patient accepts or refuses (using their advance directive and a direct, respectful conversation), and ensure this is clearly communicated to the whole team and visible in the chart/orders so blood is not given in error. (3) RESPECT the decision NON-JUDGMENTALLY — do NOT coerce, guilt, deceive, threaten, or covertly transfuse; do NOT treat the patient as incompetent simply because they refuse; and do NOT seek a court order to force blood on a COMPETENT ADULT. (4) MAXIMIZE BLOODLESS / PATIENT-BLOOD-MANAGEMENT alternatives to support the patient within their wishes: TREAT THE SOURCE of bleeding promptly and definitively (e.g., urgent ENDOSCOPY with hemostasis for an upper-GI bleed, and proton-pump inhibitor therapy; surgery/interventional radiology if needed); STIMULATE RED-CELL PRODUCTION with ERYTHROPOIETIN (EPO) plus IV IRON (and folate/B12); use ANTIFIBRINOLYTICS/hemostatic agents such as TRANEXAMIC ACID; AGGRESSIVELY MINIMIZE iatrogenic blood loss (draw the fewest, smallest specimens — pediatric/microtainer tubes, point-of-care testing, cluster labs); consider CELL SALVAGE / acute normovolemic hemodilution IF the patient accepts autologous techniques; support circulation with CRYSTALLOID/COLLOID volume; OPTIMIZE OXYGEN DELIVERY and REDUCE oxygen DEMAND (supplemental oxygen, rest, treat fever/pain, sedation if appropriate, limit exertion) so a lower hemoglobin is better tolerated; and correct any coagulopathy with agents the patient accepts. Involve a PATIENT-BLOOD-MANAGEMENT/'bloodless medicine' program, HEMATOLOGY, and the patient's resources (e.g., the Jehovah's Witness Hospital Liaison Committee, which helps connect patients with cooperative clinicians and bloodless options) and a CHAPLAIN; obtain an ETHICS CONSULT if there is conflict or uncertainty. (5) DOCUMENT the capacity assessment, the informed-refusal discussion, the specific products/techniques accepted/refused, and the plan. THE DIFFERENT RULE FOR A MINOR CHILD (and for incapacitated adults): parents may NOT refuse clearly life-saving blood for their MINOR CHILD — when a transfusion is necessary to prevent death/serious harm, the state can intervene and a COURT ORDER can authorize transfusion under the BEST-INTEREST / PARENS PATRIAE standard (parallel to overriding parental refusal of life-saving treatment — see [[case-080]]); similarly, for an adult who LACKS capacity WITHOUT a clear advance directive, emergency/implied-consent or surrogate standards apply (and a valid advance directive/no-blood card is honored even when the patient is unconscious). A competent adult refusing for HIMSELF, however, is honored. STAFF support: caring for a patient who may die from refusing an available, effective treatment causes moral distress — debrief and support the team while respecting the patient's autonomy. NURSING ROLE: assess capacity + ensure informed refusal; ask about and document the specific blood products/techniques the patient accepts or refuses and prevent inadvertent transfusion; respect the decision without coercion or covert action; aggressively pursue bloodless alternatives (treat the bleeding source, EPO + iron, tranexamic acid, minimize draws, optimize oxygen delivery) with the bloodless-medicine team; distinguish the adult-autonomy situation from the minor-child situation; and support the patient, family, and staff

patient.name: D. Coleman
patient.code: Full code WITH a documented blood-product refusal; DECISIONAL adult (capacity intact); his own decision-maker; carries a signed advance directive / 'no-blood' card; wife (also a Jehovah's Witness) supportive of his wishes; goal = stop the bleeding and support him while honoring his informed refusal of blood
patient.admitDate: Admitted with an acute UPPER-GI BLEED (melena + hematemesis, presumed bleeding peptic ulcer) and symptomatic anemia (Hgb 6.5 g/dL), borderline hemodynamics responsive to fluids. He is a JEHOVAH'S WITNESS who, with intact decision-making capacity, DECLINES transfusion of whole blood and the primary blood components (red cells, white cells, platelets, plasma) on religious grounds, and carries a signed advance directive documenting this. The team must honor his informed refusal, clarify exactly which products/techniques he will accept, and maximize bloodless management while urgently treating the bleeding source.
patient.room: Medical unit / monitored bed; GI consulted for urgent endoscopy; patient-blood-management ('bloodless medicine') team + hematology + ethics + chaplain engaged; blood-refusal status flagged prominently in the chart/orders

vitals.spo2: 97% on 2 L (supplemental O2 to optimize oxygen delivery)
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; alert + oriented + reasoning clearly; melena + prior hematemesis; Hgb 6.5 g/dL; declines blood/components per religious belief with a signed advance directive; hemodynamically borderline but stabilizing with IV crystalloid; capacity intact

37.0°C; capacity confirmed (understands his GI bleed + severe anemia, the recommendation for transfusion + its benefits, the RISKS of refusing including organ injury and DEATH, and the alternatives; reasons through it; stable choice); he reaffirms refusal of red cells/whole blood/platelets/plasma; team CLARIFIES which fractions/autologous techniques he accepts (he accepts albumin, EPO, IV iron, tranexamic acid, and cell salvage; declines the 4 primary components) and DOCUMENTS this; blood-refusal flagged in chart/orders

37.0°C; URGENT endoscopy achieves hemostasis of the bleeding ulcer (clip/cautery) + high-dose IV PPI; bloodless measures: IV iron + ERYTHROPOIETIN started, TRANEXAMIC ACID given, blood DRAWS MINIMIZED (microtainer tubes + point-of-care + clustered labs), volume support, supplemental O2 + rest to optimize oxygen delivery/reduce demand; NO blood components given (his informed refusal honored — no covert/forced transfusion); patient-blood-management team coordinating

37.0°C; no further bleeding after endoscopic hemostasis; Hgb stable (no further drop) and beginning to respond to EPO/iron; tolerating the lower hemoglobin with supportive care + activity limitation; hemodynamically stable; capacity + wishes respected; ethics/chaplain support ongoing; staff debrief for moral distress

37.0°C; bleeding controlled; hemoglobin recovering on continued EPO + iron; ambulating with improving tolerance; discharge planning with continued iron/EPO, PPI, H. pylori treatment if indicated, activity guidance, and follow-up; his autonomy + documented blood-refusal wishes were honored throughout; family + chaplain supportive

—; teaching contrast — if instead the patient were a MINOR CHILD whose parents refused a clearly life-saving transfusion, the rule DIFFERS: the state can intervene and a COURT ORDER can authorize the transfusion under the best-interest/parens patriae standard (parallel to overriding parental refusal of life-saving treatment); a competent adult refusing for HIMSELF is honored, but parents cannot refuse life-saving blood for their child

labs.v: 55 y/o man D. Coleman admitted with an acute UPPER-GI BLEED — melena and a prior episode of hematemesis, presumed from a bleeding PEPTIC ULCER — and symptomatic anemia with a HEMOGLOBIN of 6.5 g/dL; he is tachycardic (HR 108) and borderline hypotensive but responsive to IV crystalloid. He is a JEHOVAH'S WITNESS and, with intact decision-making capacity, DECLINES transfusion of whole blood and the primary blood components on religious grounds; he carries a signed ADVANCE DIRECTIVE / 'no-blood' card documenting his wishes. CORE PRINCIPLE: a COMPETENT (decisional) ADULT has the legal and ethical right to REFUSE any treatment — including a potentially life-saving blood transfusion — on religious or any other grounds, EVEN IF refusal may lead to death; AUTONOMY + informed refusal govern. Administering blood to a competent adult who has refused it (overtly or COVERTLY) constitutes battery and a serious ethical/legal violation; the team must honor his decision. PMH: peptic ulcer disease/H. pylori risk; otherwise healthy; longstanding Jehovah's Witness. SOCIAL: married; wife (also a Jehovah's Witness) present and supportive of his wishes; works full-time; connected to his faith community. The clinical task is twofold: (1) URGENTLY treat the bleeding (the most effective way to help him is to STOP the blood loss and support him without transfusion), and (2) ETHICALLY honor his informed refusal while clarifying exactly what he will accept and maximizing bloodless alternatives. This parallels the autonomy/informed-refusal framework of other competent-adult refusals (see [[case-104]] cesarean refusal, [[case-116]] capacity/dignity-of-risk) but centers on refusal of BLOOD PRODUCTS on religious grounds and the rich set of patient-blood-management alternatives. The nurse's role: confirm capacity + informed refusal, document and communicate exactly which products/techniques he accepts, prevent inadvertent transfusion, respect his choice without coercion, and drive aggressive bloodless management.
labs.r: A competent adult may refuse blood transfusion (even if life-saving) on religious/any grounds — autonomy + informed refusal govern; forced/covert transfusion = battery. The help he needs is to STOP the bleeding + support him WITHOUT blood. RN role: confirm capacity + informed refusal, honor it without coercion, clarify/document accepted products, and maximize bloodless management.

labs.n: Capacity + informed refusal + clarifying exactly what is accepted
labs.v: Two assessments anchor ethical care here: (1) DECISION-MAKING CAPACITY + INFORMED REFUSAL — confirm the patient can UNDERSTAND his condition (severe GI bleed + anemia), APPRECIATE that it applies to him, REASON through the options, and COMMUNICATE a stable choice; ensure his refusal is INFORMED, meaning the team has clearly disclosed the diagnosis, the recommended transfusion and its benefits, the serious RISKS of refusing (worsening anemia, end-organ ischemia/injury, and potentially DEATH), and the alternatives, in understandable terms; capacity is PRESUMED in adults and is NOT negated by making a choice the team considers unwise — refusing life-saving blood, by itself, does not indicate incapacity. (2) CLARIFY EXACTLY WHAT THE PATIENT WILL AND WILL NOT ACCEPT — this is essential and often misunderstood: Jehovah's Witnesses uniformly decline whole blood and the FOUR PRIMARY COMPONENTS (RED CELLS, WHITE CELLS, PLATELETS, PLASMA), but acceptance of minor BLOOD FRACTIONS (albumin, immunoglobulins, clotting factors, etc.) and of AUTOLOGOUS techniques (intraoperative CELL SALVAGE, acute normovolemic hemodilution, dialysis/bypass circuits, EPO which may contain trace albumin) is a matter of INDIVIDUAL CONSCIENCE and varies between patients — so DO NOT ASSUME; ASK the specific patient (guided by their advance directive/'no-blood' card) which products, fractions, and techniques they accept or refuse, and DOCUMENT it precisely. Then COMMUNICATE this clearly to the entire team and make it PROMINENT in the chart and orders (e.g., a 'no blood products' alert) so that blood is not transfused in error during a busy or emergent moment. Provide truthful information WITHOUT coercion, guilt, or pressure, and answer questions; involve the patient's chosen supports. THIS PATIENT had capacity confirmed and an informed refusal; he accepts albumin, EPO, IV iron, tranexamic acid, and cell salvage, while declining the four primary components — all clearly documented and flagged. The nurse's documentation of the capacity assessment, the informed-refusal discussion, and the specific accepted/refused products is critical for both ethical care and patient safety.
labs.r: Confirm CAPACITY (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate; presumed; an unwise choice ≠ incapacity) + ensure the refusal is INFORMED (disclose risks incl. death + alternatives). CLARIFY + DOCUMENT exactly which products/fractions/autologous techniques the individual accepts (Witnesses decline whole blood + the 4 primary components — RBCs/WBCs/platelets/plasma — but fractions + autologous/cell-salvage vary by conscience → ASK, don't assume) + flag prominently to prevent inadvertent transfusion. RN role: capacity + informed refusal + clarify/document/communicate accepted products.

labs.v: Because transfusion is off the table, the team must AGGRESSIVELY maximize BLOODLESS / PATIENT-BLOOD-MANAGEMENT strategies to support the patient within his wishes — and the single most important is to STOP THE BLEEDING: (1) TREAT THE SOURCE definitively and promptly — for this upper-GI bleed, URGENT ENDOSCOPY with endoscopic HEMOSTASIS (clipping, cautery, injection) plus high-dose IV PROTON-PUMP INHIBITOR therapy; escalate to interventional radiology (embolization) or surgery if bleeding is not controlled; (2) STIMULATE RED-CELL PRODUCTION — ERYTHROPOIETIN (EPO) plus IV IRON (and ensure folate/B12 adequacy) to accelerate erythropoiesis and recovery of hemoglobin; (3) REDUCE ONGOING/iatrogenic BLOOD LOSS — use ANTIFIBRINOLYTIC/hemostatic agents (TRANEXAMIC ACID) as appropriate, and AGGRESSIVELY MINIMIZE phlebotomy losses (draw the fewest and smallest specimens — pediatric/microtainer tubes, point-of-care testing, cluster lab draws, avoid unnecessary tests); (4) AUTOLOGOUS techniques IF accepted — intraoperative/peri-procedural CELL SALVAGE and acute normovolemic hemodilution (recirculating the patient's own blood in a continuous circuit is acceptable to many); (5) SUPPORT CIRCULATION with crystalloid/colloid volume to maintain perfusion; (6) OPTIMIZE OXYGEN DELIVERY and REDUCE OXYGEN DEMAND so a lower hemoglobin is tolerated — provide SUPPLEMENTAL OXYGEN, ensure REST and ACTIVITY LIMITATION, treat fever/pain/anxiety (which raise oxygen consumption), and consider sedation/ventilatory support in extreme cases; (7) CORRECT COAGULOPATHY with agents the patient accepts (e.g., vitamin K, specific factor concentrates/fractions if accepted, desmopressin) and stop/avoid anticoagulants/antiplatelets where possible; (8) consult a dedicated PATIENT-BLOOD-MANAGEMENT/'bloodless medicine' program and HEMATOLOGY early, and use the patient's resources (the Jehovah's Witness HOSPITAL LIAISON COMMITTEE can help identify cooperative clinicians/facilities and acceptable options). These measures often allow patients to survive surprisingly low hemoglobins. THIS PATIENT: urgent endoscopy achieved hemostasis + IV PPI; EPO + IV iron + tranexamic acid started; draws minimized; volume + oxygen support provided; no blood components given — and he stabilized and began recovering his hemoglobin without transfusion. The nurse coordinates and delivers these interventions, monitors for ongoing bleeding/hemodynamic change, and supports oxygenation/rest.
labs.r: Maximize BLOODLESS management: above all STOP THE BLEEDING (urgent endoscopic hemostasis + high-dose IV PPI; IR/surgery if needed); stimulate erythropoiesis (EPO + IV iron); reduce loss (tranexamic acid; MINIMIZE draws — micro tubes/POC/cluster); autologous techniques if accepted (cell salvage); volume support; OPTIMIZE oxygen delivery + REDUCE demand (O2, rest, treat fever/pain); correct coagulopathy with accepted agents; engage patient-blood-management/hematology/Liaison Committee. RN role: deliver/coordinate bloodless care + monitor.

labs.n: What NOT to do, the minor-child/incapacitated nuance, and staff support
labs.v: WHAT NOT TO DO with this competent, informed, refusing adult: do NOT TRANSFUSE blood/components against his will — overtly OR covertly (e.g., 'sneaking' blood while he is asleep or sedated is battery and a grave breach of trust/ethics/law); do NOT COERCE, guilt, shame, deceive, or pressure him; do NOT declare him incompetent simply because he refuses a life-saving treatment; do NOT seek a COURT ORDER to force transfusion on a COMPETENT ADULT (courts do not authorize forcing blood on a capacitated adult who refuses for himself); and do NOT abandon or provide substandard care because he declined blood — he receives full, aggressive bloodless care and respect. THE DIFFERENT RULE FOR A MINOR CHILD (and incapacitated adults): the analysis CHANGES when the patient cannot decide for himself. For a MINOR CHILD, parents may NOT refuse a clearly LIFE-SAVING transfusion — the state may intervene and a COURT ORDER can authorize the transfusion under the BEST-INTEREST / PARENS PATRIAE standard (the same principle that allows overriding parental refusal of life-saving treatment for a child — see [[case-080]]); the child's right to life-saving care takes precedence over the parents' religious objection in an emergency. For an ADULT who LACKS capacity: if there is a VALID ADVANCE DIRECTIVE / 'no-blood' card, it is HONORED even when the patient is unconscious; if there is NO clear directive and it is an emergency, emergency/implied-consent or the surrogate-decision-maker framework applies. A competent adult refusing for HIMSELF, however, is always honored. ETHICS + SUPPORT: obtain an ETHICS CONSULT for conflict or uncertainty; involve a CHAPLAIN and the patient's faith resources (Hospital Liaison Committee); document thoroughly. Caring for a patient who may deteriorate or die from refusing an available, effective treatment causes significant MORAL DISTRESS for staff — provide team DEBRIEFING and support while maintaining respect for the patient's autonomy (a nurse with a conscientious objection should arrange care continuity, not impose treatment). PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): capacity confirmed + refusal informed; no covert/forced transfusion; not labeled incompetent for refusing; no court order sought (competent adult); accepted products clarified/documented/flagged to prevent error; aggressive bloodless management pursued (not substandard care); and the minor-child distinction correctly understood. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: a clear advance directive + intact capacity + supportive family; prompt definitive control of the bleeding source; an engaged patient-blood-management/hematology team + Liaison Committee + ethics + chaplain; meticulous bloodless measures; and respectful, non-judgmental care with staff support. EXPECTED outcome (as here): the bleeding is controlled and the patient is supported through his anemia without transfusion, his autonomy and dignity preserved. NURSING ROLE: honor the competent adult's informed refusal without coercion or covert action, prevent inadvertent transfusion, deliver aggressive bloodless care, distinguish the minor/incapacitated situations, and support patient, family, and staff.
labs.r: Do NOT transfuse against will (overt OR covert = battery), coerce/deceive, label incompetent for refusing, or seek a court order for a COMPETENT ADULT; do not give substandard care. DIFFERENT for a MINOR CHILD: court can authorize life-saving blood over parental refusal (best-interest/parens patriae — see [[case-080]]); a valid advance directive is honored if an adult loses capacity. Ethics consult + chaplain/Liaison Committee + staff debrief (moral distress). Negatives confirm correct practice. RN role: honor refusal + bloodless care + know the minor-child distinction + support all.

nursesNote: Medical/monitored bed — 55 y/o man D. Coleman with acute UPPER-GI BLEED (melena + prior hematemesis, presumed bleeding peptic ulcer) + symptomatic anemia Hgb 6.5; HR 108, BP 104/62 (responsive to crystalloid), SpO2 97% on 2 L. JEHOVAH'S WITNESS with intact capacity who DECLINES whole blood + the 4 primary components (RBCs/WBCs/platelets/plasma) on religious grounds; carries a signed ADVANCE DIRECTIVE / 'no-blood' card; wife (also a Witness) supportive. CORE PRINCIPLE: a competent adult may refuse even life-saving transfusion (autonomy/informed refusal); forced/COVERT transfusion = battery. CAPACITY + INFORMED REFUSAL confirmed: understands the GI bleed + severe anemia, the recommendation for transfusion + benefits, the RISKS of refusing (organ injury, DEATH), and the alternatives; reasons through it; stable choice (refusing life-saving blood ≠ incapacity). CLARIFIED + DOCUMENTED exactly what he accepts/refuses (guided by his directive + direct conversation): ACCEPTS albumin, EPO, IV iron, tranexamic acid, and CELL SALVAGE; DECLINES the 4 primary components — flagged PROMINENTLY in chart/orders ('no blood products' alert) to prevent inadvertent transfusion. BLOODLESS / PATIENT-BLOOD-MANAGEMENT: above all STOP THE BLEEDING → URGENT ENDOSCOPY with hemostasis (clip/cautery) + high-dose IV PPI; EPO + IV IRON started; TRANEXAMIC ACID given; blood DRAWS MINIMIZED (microtainer tubes + point-of-care + clustered labs); volume support (crystalloid); supplemental O2 + REST/activity limitation + treat fever/pain to OPTIMIZE oxygen delivery + REDUCE demand so a lower Hgb is tolerated; patient-blood-management team + hematology + Jehovah's Witness Hospital Liaison Committee + ethics + chaplain engaged. NO blood components given (informed refusal honored — no covert/forced transfusion); NOT labeled incompetent; NO court order sought (competent adult); NOT given substandard care. DAY 2: no further bleeding after endoscopic hemostasis; Hgb stable + responding to EPO/iron; tolerating lower Hgb with supportive care; hemodynamically stable; staff DEBRIEF for moral distress (caring for a patient who could die from a refused available treatment is hard) while respecting autonomy. RECOVERY/DISCHARGE: bleeding controlled; Hgb recovering on EPO + iron; ambulating; discharge plan (continue iron/EPO, PPI, H. pylori treatment if indicated, activity guidance, follow-up); autonomy + documented wishes honored throughout. CONTRAST (teaching): if a MINOR CHILD's parents refused clearly life-saving blood, a COURT ORDER can authorize it under best-interest/parens patriae (parallel to [[case-080]]); a competent adult refusing for himself is honored, but parents cannot refuse life-saving blood for their child; a valid advance directive is honored if an adult loses capacity.
providerOrders: ADMIT to a monitored bed; acute upper-GI bleed with symptomatic anemia (Hgb 6.5) in a Jehovah's Witness who declines blood transfusion
DIAGNOSIS: acute upper-GI bleed (presumed peptic ulcer); patient declines whole blood + primary blood components on religious grounds (decisional adult with advance directive)
CODE STATUS: full code WITH documented blood-product refusal; decisional adult is his own decision-maker
BLOOD-REFUSAL / CONSENT:
Assess decision-making capacity; ensure the refusal is informed (diagnosis, recommended transfusion + benefits, risks of refusing incl. death, alternatives); document
Clarify and DOCUMENT exactly which products/fractions/autologous techniques the patient accepts vs refuses (per his advance directive + direct conversation) — ASK, do not assume
Flag the blood-refusal status PROMINENTLY in the chart and orders (e.g., 'NO blood products' alert) to prevent inadvertent transfusion; communicate to the entire team
Do NOT transfuse blood/components against his will, overtly or covertly; do not coerce/deceive/guilt; do not label him incompetent for refusing; do not seek a court order for a competent adult
STOP THE BLEEDING (definitive source control):
Urgent GI consult for endoscopy with hemostasis (clip/cautery/injection); high-dose IV proton-pump inhibitor; escalate to interventional radiology (embolization) or surgery if uncontrolled
MAXIMIZE BLOODLESS / PATIENT-BLOOD-MANAGEMENT:
Erythropoietin (EPO) + IV iron (ensure folate/B12) to stimulate red-cell production

Tranexamic acid / hemostatic agents as appropriate; correct coagulopathy with accepted agents (vitamin K, factor concentrates/fractions if accepted, desmopressin); hold/avoid anticoagulants/antiplatelets where possible
Minimize iatrogenic blood loss: fewest/smallest specimens (pediatric/microtainer tubes), point-of-care testing, cluster labs, avoid unnecessary draws
Cell salvage / acute normovolemic hemodilution IF the patient accepts autologous techniques
Volume support (crystalloid/colloid) to maintain perfusion; supplemental oxygen + rest/activity limitation + treat fever/pain/anxiety to optimize oxygen delivery and reduce demand
Consult the patient-blood-management/'bloodless medicine' program + hematology; engage the patient's resources (Jehovah's Witness Hospital Liaison Committee)
MONITORING:
Vital signs + hemodynamics + signs of ongoing/recurrent bleeding (melena/hematemesis, HR/BP, mental status, urine output); serial hemoglobin (minimizing draw volume); telemetry as indicated
ETHICS + SUPPORT + DOCUMENTATION:
Ethics consult for conflict/uncertainty; chaplain + faith resources; respect autonomy non-judgmentally
Support staff moral distress (debrief); a conscientiously objecting nurse arranges care continuity rather than imposing treatment
Document the capacity assessment, informed-refusal discussion, specific accepted/refused products, and the bloodless plan
MINOR/INCAPACITATED NUANCE (awareness):

For a MINOR child whose parents refuse clearly life-saving blood, the state can intervene and a court order can authorize transfusion (best-interest/parens patriae); a valid advance directive is honored if an adult loses capacity; a competent adult refusing for himself is honored
DISCHARGE PLANNING:
Continue iron/EPO as indicated; PPI + H. pylori testing/treatment if indicated; activity guidance; outpatient follow-up; reinforce documented blood-product wishes

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters with this bleeding patient who refuses blood?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically and ethically significant.

opts.text: A competent adult has the right to refuse a blood transfusion (even if life-saving) on religious or any grounds — autonomy and informed refusal govern, and his decision must be honored
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the core ethical/legal principle for a competent adult. RN role: respect informed refusal.

opts.text: Confirm decision-making capacity and ensure the refusal is informed (he understands the risks including death and the alternatives) — refusing a recommended treatment does not equal incapacity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — capacity + informed refusal validate the decision. RN role: capacity assessment + informed disclosure.

opts.text: Clarify and document exactly which products/fractions/autologous techniques he accepts vs refuses (ask the individual; many accept albumin/EPO/cell salvage) and flag it prominently to prevent inadvertent transfusion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — individual variation in accepted products + error prevention. RN role: clarify/document/flag accepted products.

opts.text: The most effective help is to STOP the bleeding — urgent endoscopy with hemostasis + high-dose IV PPI for this upper-GI bleed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — definitive source control is the priority intervention. RN role: facilitate urgent endoscopy + PPI.

opts.text: Maximize bloodless alternatives — EPO + IV iron, tranexamic acid, minimize blood draws, volume support, and optimize oxygen delivery (supplemental O2, rest)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — patient-blood-management supports him within his wishes. RN role: deliver/coordinate bloodless measures.

opts.text: Engage the patient-blood-management/bloodless team, hematology, ethics, chaplain, and the patient's faith resources; support the patient and family
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multidisciplinary + faith-sensitive support. RN role: mobilize resources + support.

opts.text: Know the different rule for a minor child (a court can authorize life-saving blood over parental refusal) vs a competent adult (his refusal is honored)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the adult-vs-minor distinction is high-yield + decisive. RN role: apply the correct standard.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Because his hemoglobin is dangerously low and refusal could be fatal, transfuse red cells covertly while he is asleep to save his life
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + illegal/unethical. Covertly transfusing a competent adult who refused is BATTERY and a grave violation of autonomy and trust. Honor the refusal; maximize bloodless management and treat the bleeding source.

opts.text: Obtain an emergency court order to compel transfusion of this competent, refusing adult for his own good
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Courts do not authorize forcing blood on a competent adult who refuses for himself (that standard applies to a minor child). Respect his informed refusal.

opts.text: A competent adult's informed refusal of transfusion is legally and ethically binding even if it may result in death; forced or covert transfusion constitutes battery
opts.rat: CORRECT — the autonomy/battery principle. Foundation for honoring the refusal.

opts.text: Jehovah's Witnesses decline whole blood and the four primary components (red cells, white cells, platelets, plasma), but acceptance of minor fractions and autologous techniques varies by individual conscience — so ask each patient
opts.rat: CORRECT — what is/ isn't refused + individual variation. Foundation for clarifying accepted products.

opts.text: The most effective intervention is definitive control of the bleeding source (endoscopic hemostasis + IV PPI), alongside bloodless measures (EPO/iron, tranexamic acid, minimize draws, optimize oxygen delivery)
opts.rat: CORRECT — source control + patient-blood-management framework. Foundation for the clinical plan.

opts.text: Capacity is presumed in adults and is not negated by refusing a recommended treatment; an informed, capacitated refusal must be respected without coercion
opts.rat: CORRECT — capacity principle. Foundation for validating the refusal.

opts.text: For a minor child, parents may not refuse clearly life-saving blood — a court can authorize transfusion under best-interest/parens patriae; a valid advance directive is honored if an adult loses capacity
opts.rat: CORRECT — the minor/incapacitated distinction. Foundation for applying the right standard.

opts.text: Engaging a patient-blood-management program, hematology, and the patient's faith resources, plus documenting/flagging accepted products, supports safe, respectful care
opts.rat: CORRECT — multidisciplinary + safety/documentation principle. Foundation for coordinated care.

opts.text: Refusing a life-saving transfusion is evidence that the patient lacks decision-making capacity and a surrogate should consent to transfusion on his behalf
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. An unwise/risky choice does not equal incapacity; a competent adult's refusal stands and is not overridden by a surrogate. Capacity is assessed by its real criteria.

opts.text: Once blood is refused, there is little else to offer, so care should be minimized while awaiting deterioration
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Aggressive bloodless management (stop the bleeding, EPO/iron, tranexamic acid, minimize draws, oxygen optimization) is offered — refusing blood does not mean substandard or withdrawn care.

opts.text: Stop the bleeding — urgent endoscopic hemostasis + high-dose IV PPI (definitive source control), with hemodynamic/bleeding monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Controlling the blood loss is the most effective, life-saving intervention available within his wishes. RN role: facilitate source control + monitor.

opts.text: Confirm capacity + informed refusal and clarify/document/flag exactly which products and techniques he accepts vs refuses
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Validates the decision and prevents inadvertent transfusion. RN role: capacity + informed refusal + documentation/flag.

opts.text: Maximize bloodless management — EPO + IV iron, tranexamic acid, minimize draws, volume support, optimize oxygen delivery/reduce demand
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Supports the patient through anemia and recovery without transfusion. RN role: deliver/coordinate bloodless care.

opts.text: Respect autonomy without coercion or covert action; engage ethics, chaplain, and the patient's faith resources
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Honoring the decision + appropriate support uphold ethics and trust. RN role: respect + mobilize support.

opts.text: Support staff moral distress and document thoroughly; understand the minor-child/incapacitated distinction
opts.rat: #5 — Important. Team support + documentation + correct legal framework complete the care. RN role: support + document + apply the right standard.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient.

opts.text: Facilitate urgent endoscopy with hemostasis and start high-dose IV PPI to control the bleeding source
opts.rat: Definitive source control is the priority. RN role: facilitate endoscopy + PPI.

opts.text: Confirm capacity, ensure informed refusal, and clarify/document/flag exactly which products and autologous techniques he accepts or refuses
opts.rat: Validates the decision + prevents inadvertent transfusion. RN role: capacity + clarify/document.

opts.text: Start EPO + IV iron, give tranexamic acid, minimize blood draws (micro tubes/POC/cluster), and provide volume + oxygen support with rest
opts.rat: Core bloodless/patient-blood-management measures. RN role: deliver/coordinate.

opts.text: Engage the patient-blood-management program, hematology, ethics, chaplain, and the Jehovah's Witness Hospital Liaison Committee
opts.rat: Multidisciplinary + faith-sensitive support. RN role: mobilize resources.

opts.text: Respect his informed refusal non-judgmentally and support staff experiencing moral distress
opts.rat: Upholds autonomy + sustains the team. RN role: respect + team support.

opts.text: Document the capacity assessment, informed-refusal discussion, accepted/refused products, and the bloodless plan
opts.rat: Essential for ethics + safety. RN role: thorough documentation.

opts.text: Transfuse red cells covertly while he sleeps because his hemoglobin is dangerously low
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/illegal — covert transfusion of a refusing competent adult is battery. Honor the refusal; maximize bloodless care.

opts.text: Obtain a court order to force transfusion of this competent, refusing adult
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — courts do not compel transfusion of a competent adult refusing for himself (that applies to a minor child). Respect his decision.

opts.text: Declare him incompetent because he is refusing a life-saving treatment, and have a surrogate consent to blood
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — refusing a recommended treatment is not incapacity; his capacitated refusal stands and is not overridden by a surrogate.

opts.text: Minimize his care and simply wait for him to deteriorate since he refused the definitive treatment
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — refusing blood does not mean substandard/withdrawn care; provide aggressive bloodless management and source control.

opts.text: Pressure and guilt him about leaving his family if he dies, to change his mind about the transfusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — coercion/guilt/manipulation violates autonomy and trust. Provide truthful information without pressure and respect his decision.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stabilize + facilitate definitive source control — volume support/oxygen, monitor hemodynamics/bleeding, and expedite urgent endoscopy with IV PPI
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stopping the bleeding (with stabilization) is the most life-saving action within his wishes. RN role: stabilize + facilitate source control.

opts.text: Confirm capacity + informed refusal and clarify/document/flag exactly which products and techniques he accepts vs refuses
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Validates the decision and prevents inadvertent transfusion before other care proceeds. RN role: capacity + clarify/document/flag.

opts.text: Implement bloodless management — EPO + IV iron, tranexamic acid, minimize draws, optimize oxygen delivery + rest
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Support the patient through anemia/recovery without transfusion. RN role: bloodless measures.

opts.text: Engage the patient-blood-management team, hematology, ethics, chaplain, and faith resources; respect autonomy without coercion
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Coordinated, faith-sensitive support upholds the plan and ethics. RN role: mobilize resources + respect.

opts.text: Document thoroughly, support staff moral distress, and ensure follow-up; apply the correct framework if a minor/incapacitated situation arises
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/after. Documentation + team support + correct legal framework complete the care. RN role: document + support + apply standards.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action taken, classify as Appropriate or Inappropriate.

opts.text: The team confirmed capacity + informed refusal, clarified that he accepts albumin/EPO/iron/tranexamic acid/cell salvage but declines the four primary components, and flagged this prominently
opts.rat: Correct capacity/informed-refusal assessment + individualized clarification + error prevention. Model handling.

opts.text: Urgent endoscopy achieved hemostasis with high-dose IV PPI, and bloodless measures (EPO/iron, tranexamic acid, minimized draws, oxygen/rest) were implemented
opts.rat: Definitive source control + aggressive patient-blood-management. Model handling.

opts.text: His informed refusal was respected non-judgmentally, with ethics, chaplain, and the Hospital Liaison Committee engaged and staff supported
opts.rat: Respect for autonomy + appropriate support resources. Model handling.

opts.text: A nurse, believing it was for his own good, transfused red cells covertly while he slept
opts.rat: ⚠️ Covert transfusion of a refusing competent adult is battery and a grave ethical/legal violation. Never appropriate.

opts.text: The team sought a court order to force transfusion on this competent, refusing adult
opts.rat: ⚠️ Courts do not compel transfusion of a competent adult refusing for himself (that applies to a minor child). Incorrect.

opts.text: Staff documented him as 'incompetent' for refusing and had his wife consent to blood on his behalf
opts.rat: ⚠️ Refusing a recommended treatment is not incapacity; a surrogate cannot override a capacitated adult's refusal. Incorrect.

opts.text: Believing nothing more could be done, the team minimized care and waited for deterioration
opts.rat: ⚠️ Refusing blood does not mean withdrawn/substandard care; aggressive bloodless management + source control are indicated. Incorrect.

opts.text: Over two days the bleeding stayed controlled and his hemoglobin stabilized and began recovering on EPO + iron without any transfusion
opts.rat: Expected favorable outcome of source control + patient-blood-management while honoring his wishes. Model outcome.

opts.text: The nurse pressured and guilted him about his family to try to change his mind about the transfusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ Coercion/guilt violates autonomy and trust. Provide information without pressure and respect his decision. Incorrect.

opts.text: In a contrasting case, a court order was obtained to authorize a life-saving transfusion for a MINOR child whose parents refused on religious grounds
opts.rat: Correct application of the different standard for a minor child (best-interest/parens patriae) — parents cannot refuse clearly life-saving blood for their child. Model handling of the contrasting situation.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
